title: Blocking SQLite Mutex held across queries on async runtime (starvation under contention)
description:
Current state: 

Desired state: 

Reason: 

Dimension: Performance & Complexity (6) / Architectural Alignment (2)

Location: src/memory.rs:105-108, every public method uses self.conn.lock().unwrap(); src/prompt.rs:192,204 calls db.format_learned_preferences() and format_recent_context_for_prompt() from sync prompt builders that run inside tokio async context.

Violation: MemoryDb wraps rusqlite::Connection in std::sync::Mutex. Every method takes the blocking lock via .lock().unwrap() and holds it across I/O-bound SQLite calls (FTS search, joins, execute_batch with triggers). When called from async code paths (prompt building runs on the tokio runtime before each turn in pipeline.rs), the thread blocks inside lock + blocks inside SQLite. With multiple concurrent agent workers or VDD subagents sharing the Arc<MemoryDb>, a long-running FTS search starves every other async task on the same runtime worker.

Additional issues:
- .lock().unwrap() panics on mutex poisoning instead of recovering (28 call sites).
- No transactions anywhere. save_coding_pattern (line 908) does SELECT then UPDATE or INSERT — classic read-modify-write race: two concurrent callers can both see 'no existing row' and both INSERT, violating intended upsert semantics. Same bug in save_error_pattern (1026), save_learned_preference (1120), save_file_relationship uses ON CONFLICT but the others do not.

Mandated Refactor:
1. Switch to tokio::sync::Mutex OR wrap every DB op in tokio::task::spawn_blocking at the call-site layer. Blocking sync mutex + sync SQLite inside async is an architectural smell.
2. Wrap each read-modify-write sequence in a transaction (conn.transaction()?) with SQLite default deferred mode or IMMEDIATE for writes.
3. Use ON CONFLICT ... DO UPDATE (true upsert) everywhere save_coding_pattern / save_error_pattern / save_learned_preference manually SELECT-then-INSERT.
4. Replace .unwrap() on poisoned lock with .map_err to coerce into anyhow::Error with context 'memory mutex poisoned'.
5. Long-term: wrap MemoryDb in a command channel (actor) so only one thread ever touches the connection — standard pattern for rusqlite in async apps.
